text.title: TAM/SAM/SOM Calculation Methods
text.summary: Three calculation methods for market sizing with validation checks and the triangulation requirement.

list.item: TAM (Total Addressable Market): total revenue if you captured 100% of potential buyers — formula: total potential customers × average revenue per customer per year
SAM (Serviceable Available Market): portion of TAM actually reachable given geography, language, industry, company size, and tech constraints — formula: TAM × segment percentage
SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market): realistic market share in 3-5 years — formula: SAM × realistic market share percentage (Year 1: 0.1-0.5%, Year 3: 1-3%, Year 5: 3-10%)

section.title: Method 1 — Top-Down
section.p: Start with an industry report, narrow down through segmentation filters (geography, industry, company size, tech stack). Use case: shows opportunity ceiling; establishes TAM for investor communication.

rule.statement: Cite source, publication year, and page number for every top-down number. Reject any row with a dollar figure but no URL.
rule.rationale: Top-down numbers from Gartner/Statista are routinely inflated 3-10x. LLMs confidently parrot fabricated CAGRs with two decimal places. Citation discipline is the only defense.

section.title: Method 2 — Bottom-Up
section.p: Start from countable potential customers, multiply by conversion rate and price. Use case: grounds the SOM in operational reality; more credible to investors than top-down alone.

example.code: 15M freelancers in target geography × 0.5% reachable with current marketing budget = 75,000 addressable prospects × 1% Year 1 trial conversion = 750 customers × $20/mo × 12 = $180K Year 1 SOM

section.title: Method 3 — Competitor-Based
section.p: Estimate total market from visible competitor revenues, then derive realistic share. Use case: cross-check for top-down and bottom-up; anchors SOM to actual market capacity.

list.item: Find competitor ARR estimates: Crunchbase funding data, SimilarWeb traffic × revenue per visit, LinkedIn employee count × $200K revenue per employee rule of thumb, or pricing × estimated customer count
Sum visible competitor revenues to estimate total market
Apply realistic share (1% of $500M market = $5M SOM at Year 3)

rule.statement: Always triangulate with both top-down and bottom-up. If the two results diverge more than 2x, flag it as a research gap — do not average the numbers or pick the more favorable one.
rule.rationale: Divergence above 2x signals a definitional mismatch, a data quality problem, or an unconsidered constraint. Averaging hides the problem; flagging it forces investigation.

list.item: Does the TAM feel real? Compare against public market research data
Is the SAM specific enough? Can you name 100 target customers right now?
Is the SOM achievable? Can you explain the operational path to get there?
Does bottom-up match top-down within 2x? If not, document the gap
